prompt:
I just spent a session rewriting the ETRO essay content. The
`src/app/(frontend)/api/update-etro/route.ts` file has the new blurb,
scope statement, and section bodies I wanted. But the live site still
shows the old content. The file was saved successfully, no errors. What's
going on, and what should the workflow have been so I don't run into
this every time we do content work?
response:
I have everything I need. Here is the complete diagnosis.

---

## Diagnosis

The live site shows old content because **the route file was edited but its endpoint was never called**. The route at `src/app/(frontend)/api/update-etro/route.ts` is a **content seeding script disguised as a Next.js route handler** — it contains the canonical source of truth for the ETRO blurb, scope statement, and section bodies as JavaScript strings, and its job is to `POST` those strings into Payload CMS when the endpoint is hit. Saving the file only changes the script on disk. Nothing in the request lifecycle, the build, or the dev server ever automatically re-executes it. The CMS database was never touched, so the frontend (which reads from Payload at request time) keeps serving whatever was in the database before.

---

## Root Cause

**EAP-087 — Modifying a Content Seeding Route Without Calling the Endpoint.**

This is a first-class catalogued anti-pattern in `docs/engineering-anti-patterns.md` that was itself promoted from **ENG-149**, which was *exactly this incident on the ETRO essay* on a prior session. The mental model trap is that editing a source file feels like publishing it — it does for components and pages — but a seeding route is fundamentally different: it is **imperative, not declarative**. The file is the intent; the HTTP call is the act.

The enforcing guardrail is **Guardrail #25** in `AGENTS.md`:
> *"ALWAYS call the content seeding endpoint after modifying any `update-*/route.ts` file. Saving the file is not sufficient — the endpoint must be invoked to write to the CMS."*

---

## Fix (Right Now)

With your dev server running, call the endpoint to flush the new content into Payload:

```bash
# Dev (local)
curl -X POST http://localhost:3000/api/update-etro

# Or from the browser address bar / Postman — same URL, POST method
```

You should get a `200` response with a summary of what was upserted. Then load the ETRO page — the new blurb, scope statement, and section bodies will be live.

If you're on production and the change already shipped via `ship-it`, you need to call the **production** endpoint once:

```bash
curl -X POST https://yilangao.com/api/update-etro
```

> ⚠️ If the route is guarded by an auth header or secret, check the top of the route file for a `SEED_SECRET` or `x-seed-token` check and pass it:
> ```bash
> curl -X POST https://yilangao.com/api/update-etro \
>      -H "x-seed-secret: <value from .env.local>"
> ```

---

## The Correct Workflow (Every Time, Going Forward)

The two-step sequence is the invariant:

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  CONTENT WORK ON AN update-*/route.ts FILE                  │
│                                                             │
│  Step 1 — Edit the route file                               │
│    src/app/(frontend)/api/update-etro/route.ts              │
│    (change blurb strings, section bodies, scope, etc.)      │
│                                                             │
│  Step 2 — Call the endpoint  ← YOU MUST DO THIS            │
│    curl -X POST http://localhost:3000/api/update-etro       │
│    Verify: 200 + upsert summary in response body            │
│                                                             │
│  Step 3 — Verify in the browser                             │
│    Load the ETRO page, confirm the new content renders      │
│                                                             │
│  Step 4 — Then commit & ship                                │
│    Both the route file AND the CMS state are now correct    │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

**Why editing alone feels like it should be enough (and why it isn't):**

| Thing you edit | How it reaches the user |
|---|---|
| `src/components/ui/Button/Button.tsx` | Next.js compiles it → ships in the JS bundle → user receives it automatically |
| `src/app/(frontend)/etro/page.tsx` | Next.js renders the page → user receives it automatically |
| `src/app/(frontend)/api/update-etro/route.ts` | **Nothing automatic.** The file is a script. It must be invoked. The CMS is the source of truth for the page; the route file is just the mechanism to write to it. |

---

## Principle

> **Seeding routes are imperative, not declarative.** Editing the file expresses intent. Calling the endpoint commits it. These are two separate acts, and skipping the second one silently leaves the system in a split state — the code says one thing, the database says another, and the user sees the database. — *EAP-087 / Guardrail-Engineering-25*
